summary: Gruber criticizes Apple's App Store review process for repeatedly rejecting Ice Cubes, a well-crafted native Mastodon client built in SwiftUI, for nonsensical reasons including claims it lacks 'minimum functionality' and doesn't differ from a web browsing experience. He draws a parallel between the current Mastodon client boom and the early Twitter client era, noting that Mastodon's truly open APIs have created a new UI design playground on iOS. Gruber argues that Ice Cubes exemplifies everything Apple encourages developers to build — native, platform-specific, painstakingly designed — making the rejection particularly absurd. He calls the reviewers an embarrassment to Apple and warns they are locking the gates on a new creative frontier. In a telling postscript, Ice Cubes was approved roughly two hours after Gruber published the piece.
key_points: Mastodon's open APIs have created a new UI design playground reminiscent of early Twitter, but without the risk of platform lock-out
Ice Cubes was rejected seven times in six days under guideline 4.2.2 ('Minimum Functionality'), despite being a fully native SwiftUI app embracing iOS design idioms
The rejection directly contradicts Apple's own stated values of encouraging native app development over web wrappers
App Store review's faceless bureaucracy actively undermines Apple's reputation as a platform steward that respects independent developers
The post itself apparently pressured Apple into approving the app within two hours of publication
money_quotes: Today, Mastodon's explosive growth in the face of Twitter's collapse has made it a new UI playground, especially so on iOS.
Mastodon has that small-nugget timeline nature as Twitter, but is a truly open platform.
There are no limits to what developers can choose to do with the Mastodon APIs. There are, however, limits to what iOS developers can deliver to users: App Store review.
It is the complete opposite of a thin wrapper around a web app — it is truly native, painstakingly designed, and built using Apple's avowed framework of the future, SwiftUI.

Native apps are what make Apple's platforms stand apart, yet the App Store reviewer(s) repeatedly rejecting Ice Cubes apparently think iOS and Mac users are better off using the same cross-platform web apps available on Android and Windows and Chromebooks.
An App Store reviewer who cannot see how Ice Cubes 'differ[s] from a mobile web browsing experience' is an embarrassment to the company.
It's Apple's utterly clueless bureaucratic App Store reviewers who are doing their best to lock the new playground's gates before they even open.
key_insight: Apple's App Store review process is so broken that it actively punishes the kind of native, platform-specific development Apple itself champions, undermining its own ecosystem at exactly the moment a new creative frontier is opening up.
